← all tutorials

Vector Clocks (Mattern 1989, Fidge 1988) — interactive 📖 VECTOR_CLOCKS.md 📄 vector_clocks.py

📖 Full guide  ·  📄 Ground-truth source — every panel below recomputes the same model in JS.
Three processes, each keeping a vector of 3 counters. Watch the rules fire on local / send / receive events. Click two events to compare them — the page classifies the pair as a→b (causal), b→a, or a ‖ b (concurrent), and walks the comparison component by component. Everything is recomputed in JS from the identical rules as vector_clocks.py.  |  conflict resolution →

① The 3-process timeline — local / send / receive rules, live

What to do: click an event to select it (it turns gold), then click a second event to compare. The vector clock after each event is shown beneath it. Arrows are messages (they ship a COPY of the sender's whole vector).
preset pairs:
P0 / send selected event causal link (a→b) concurrent (a‖b)
Click two events to compare their vector clocks.

② Component-by-component comparison — how VC(a) < VC(b) is decided

The rule: VC(a) < VC(b) iff every component a[k] ≤ b[k] AND at least one a[k] < b[k]. A single slot with a[k] > b[k] sinks the whole comparison → that direction is impossible → if BOTH directions are sunk, the events are concurrent.

③ Why Lamport can't do this — same events, scalar clock fails to detect ‖

The trap: a scalar Lamport clock gives a→b ⇒ L(a)<L(b) but not the converse. The concurrent pair a2 ‖ c1 gets L(c1)=1 < L(a2)=2 — a naive reader infers c1→a2, which is wrong. Toggle the clock model; the vector clock fixes it because each slot records what the process has heard.

④ DynamoDB version vectors — concurrent writes become sibling conflicts

Same comparison idea, data-store flavor: increment OWN node on a WRITE, merge (max) on read. Two versions of a key whose version vectors are concurrent are siblings surfaced to the app — never silently dropped.
check: … JS recompute matches vector_clocks.py GOLD (all 56 ordered pairs classify correctly).
